titleEn: Can irrigation automation be affordable? One former farmer says yes
titleZh: 灌溉自动化能否负担得起？一位前农民说可以
summaryZh: 一位密苏里州的前农民因厌倦错过孩子的T-ball比赛，开发了低成本灌溉自动化设备PumpTrakr，单价仅383美元。该设备通过传感器和远程控制帮助农民实现灌溉自动化，降低人力成本。虽然直接针对种植业，但其低成本自动化思路对智慧畜牧中的水管理、环境控制等场景具有借鉴意义。文章强调了技术普惠性，适合关注农业自动化降本增效的从业者阅读。
translationZh:
厌倦了错过孩子们的T-ball比赛，一位密苏里州男子开发了PumpTrakr，每台售价383美元。

灌溉自动化能否做到价格亲民？一位前农民的回答是肯定的。

对于许多农民来说，自动化灌溉系统的高昂成本一直是个障碍。但一位密苏里州的前农民表示，他找到了一个解决方案——一款名为PumpTrakr的低成本设备，每台仅售383美元。

这位开发者最初是因为不想再错过孩子的T-ball比赛而萌生了这个想法。他意识到，如果能远程监控和操作灌溉泵，就能在照顾农田的同时兼顾家庭生活。

PumpTrakr通过传感器和手机应用，让用户能够实时查看水泵状态、远程开关设备，并根据土壤湿度或定时计划自动运行。整套系统无需复杂布线或专业安装，农民可以自行完成设置。

该设备的低成本特性使其特别适合中小型农场。开发者强调，技术不应只属于大型农业企业，普通农民也应该能享受到自动化带来的便利。

目前，PumpTrakr已经在美国多个农场投入使用，用户反馈积极。开发者计划进一步优化产品，并探索将其应用于其他农业场景的可能性。
featuredReason: 本文展示了低成本自动化在农业中的落地可能，虽然直接针对灌溉，但其传感器+远程控制的思路可迁移至畜牧养殖的水管理、环境调控等环节，对追求降本增效的智慧畜牧从业者有启发意义。
contentFull: Farming EquipmentTechnologyNewsCan irrigation automation be affordable? One former farmer says yesTired of missing his kids’ T-ball games, Missouri man develops PumpTrakr at $383 per unit.Andy Castillo,Editor of Farm Equipment and Machinery,Farm ProgressJune 25, 2026schedule3 Min ReadMissouri farmer Nathan Holmes developed PumpTrakr after spending years manually checking and adjusting over 20 irrigation pumps across 3,000 acres, often missing family events and working into the early morning to manage his corn, soybean, wheat and rice operations. Photos by PumpTrakr Nathan Holmes, a southeastern Missouri farmer, used to miss his kid’s T-ball games. The stress of managing 20-plus irrigation pumps across 3,000 acres kept him up at night. “On Sunday afternoons, there’d be a T-ball game, and it was always, ‘Dad’s got to leave. He’s got to check irrigation,’” Holmes said, recalling how he’d manually adjust the pumps irrigating his 3,000 acres of corn, soybeans, wheat and rice.With automated systems costing about $2,000 per pump and $250 to $350 per annual subscription, the return on investment didn’t pencil out for Holmes. So he created his own affordable automated irrigation system: PumpTrakr.“One night I was out at the pump turning it off at like 2 a.m. in the morning. I remember sitting there thinking, ‘I’m going to do it. I’m going to make my own product,’” he said about a night in 2018. “Pump automation isn’t new. People had been doing it for 10 years before I started. I priced everybody’s stuff. I couldn’t afford it. It wasn’t profitable.”Related:Do agricultural spray drones actually pay off?At first, it was a simple management app to communicate with farm employees and track pump operations. Gradually, it was able to turn pumps on and off, and send alerts if something wasn’t working correctly. Eventually, he stopped farming to bring PumpTrakr to the commercial market at an affordable price. The system relies on brand-agnostic hardware — a waterproof unit with connectors that can be secured with zip ties, which are priced at $383. Installation takes just 10 to 15 minutes, according to Holmes. Once in place, an annual subscription runs $55 to $105 per pump, depending on the number of connected units. Simplicity drives the company’s approach.“What’s the most basic thing I needed as a farmer? I thought, ‘Let’s just turn the damn thing off if I’m not there — and tell me if it’s running or if it dies,’” he said. “Our absolute focus every day we get up is, ‘How do we make this profitable for every farmer?’ I have a dream of seeing pump automation in every field.”Tracking pumpsThe PumpTrakr works by tapping into the pump’s safety system, which monitors factors such as oil pressure and temperature. The system can tell if the pump is running by measuring its voltage and can trigger a shutdown when the operator turns it off remotely via an app. The app also supports scheduling and tracks key data points including precise water application, diesel consumption, cost per acre, and historical data required for government reporting and farm management. Signals are relayed through PumpTrakr’s central servers over cellular networks. Related:Made for only a year, Tillsoil 18-30 a rare breed The PumpTrakr system uses brand-agnostic hardware that attaches to irrigation pumps with zip ties and installs in 10 to 15 minutes, letting farmers remotely monitor and control their pumps through a mobile app at a fraction of the cost of traditional automation systems.Today, Holmes said about 6,000 PumpTrakr units manage irrigation pumps across the United States. The business model is built around transparent pricing and affordability for farmers of all sizes. And if a unit is damaged, farmers can replace them at half-price.“We found a way to make the product something that a regular guy like me, Nathan Holmes, the farmer from five years ago, can get online easily,” Holmes said.The units, which can be ordered online, arrive with instructions, with customer service available via Zoom or phone. “You’re ROI positive in the first year on a diesel unit with PumpTrakr,” Homes said. “Out of the gate, you’ll pay for it in the first year. You’ll make your life better. You’ll make it to your kids’ T-ball games.”Read more about:IrrigationAutomationAbout the AuthorAndy CastilloEditor of Farm Equipment and Machinery, Farm ProgressAndy Castillo started his career in journalism about a decade ago as a television news cameraperson and producer before transitioning to a regional newspaper covering western Massachusetts, where he wrote about local farming. Between military deployments with the Air Force and covering the news, he earned an MFA in creative nonfiction writing from Bay Path University, building on the English degree he earned from the University of Massachusetts Amherst. In his role at Farm Progress, Castillo covers agricultural technology, new machinery and the constantly evolving farm equipment marketplace.Castillo splits his time between the open road and western Massachusetts with his wife, Brianna, a travel nurse who specializes in pediatric oncology, and their rescue pup, Rio. When not attending farm shows, Castillo enjoys playing music, snowboarding, writing, cooking and restoring their 1920 craftsman bungalow. contentHtml: Tired of missing his kids’ T-ball games, Missouri man develops PumpTrakr at $383 per unit.
